blocks.text: Chapter 8.
Relaxation and Randomness: Solving the Unsolvable
The ideas in 30 seconds
Some problems are intractable: no algorithm can guarantee the best answer in reasonable time, so the rational move is to stop demanding perfection and optimize differently.

Relaxation means deliberately solving an easier version of your problem — removing constraints, treating discrete choices as continuous, or converting hard rules into prices — then using that answer as a bound or a starting point, often with a mathematical guarantee of how close you'll land.

Randomness is a legitimate computational strategy, not a failure of rigor: sampling (Monte Carlo) can beat exhaustive analysis, and randomized tests like Miller-Rabin trade a vanishingly small chance of error for enormous speed.
Sketchy-but-fast data structures like Bloom filters show that accepting a controlled false-positive rate can shrink a problem by orders of magnitude.

When searching a rugged solution landscape, pure improvement gets stuck at local maxima; jitter, random restarts, and simulated annealing — being tolerant of bad moves early and increasingly strict later — reliably find better answers, in chip design and in life decisions alike.
Intractability: When the Best Answer Is Officially Out of Reach

Consider a salesman who must visit a set of towns once each and return home by the shortest route — a problem Abraham Lincoln effectively lived as a circuit-riding lawyer in Illinois. This traveling salesman problem, formalized by mathematician Merrill Flood in the 1930s, looks innocent but explodes: the number of possible routes grows factorially, and decades of research strongly suggest no algorithm can guarantee the optimal tour without essentially trying them all.

Computer science draws the line of "solvable in practice" using the Cobham–Edmonds thesis: a problem is tractable only if it can be solved in polynomial time (n², n³, and so on, for input size n). Problems whose best known solutions require exponential or factorial time are intractable — a modest increase in size overwhelms any conceivable hardware. The traveling salesman problem belongs to the notorious class of NP-hard problems, where verifying an answer is easy but finding the best one appears fundamentally hard; unless the famous P = NP conjecture resolves in an unexpected direction, no clever trick will ever make these problems fast.

This is liberating rather than depressing. Scheduling with precedence constraints (chapter 5), seating charts, route planning, and countless real decisions are provably NP-hard. When your problem is intractable, agonizing over the perfect answer isn't diligence — it's a category error. The professionals who work on these problems daily don't seek perfection; they use two families of techniques that trade a small, often quantifiable amount of quality for astronomical savings in effort: relaxation and randomness.
The Relaxation Toolkit: Solve the Easier Problem First

Constraint relaxation removes a rule that makes the problem hard, solves the easier version, then translates back. Relax the traveling salesman's requirement to visit each town exactly once in a closed loop, and you get the minimum spanning tree — the cheapest network connecting all towns — which is computable quickly. The tree's cost is a lower bound on the best possible tour, and tracing around it yields a real tour guaranteed to be at most twice optimal; in practice, spanning-tree-based tours often come within a few percent of perfect.

Continuous relaxation attacks discrete problems by pretending fractions are allowed. Suppose you're sending party invitations and want the smallest set of people such that everyone you care about knows an invitee — the NP-hard minimum vertex cover problem. Allow yourself to invite someone "half-way," and the fractional version becomes efficiently solvable with linear programming. Then round the fractions to real decisions. Rounding schemes carry proofs: for vertex cover, rounding every fraction of one-half or more up to a full invitation yields a guest list at most twice the true optimum. Randomized rounding — treating a 0.6 as a 60% chance of inviting — gives similar guarantees elsewhere. You can't easily do better, but you know exactly how much worse you might be.

Lagrangian relaxation does something subtler: it takes hard constraints and turns them into costs. Instead of "this is impossible," the rule becomes "this is expensive" — violate it if you must, but pay a penalty in the objective function. Operations researcher Michael Trick, who schedules Major League Baseball and NCAA basketball seasons, relies on it because real scheduling problems are over-constrained: satisfying every stadium, broadcaster, and league rule simultaneously is impossible, so some constraints must become negotiable prices. The life lesson is direct — when your to-do list is infeasible, the answer isn't paralysis but deciding which "musts" are actually penalties you're willing to absorb. As the authors put it, unless we're willing to spend eons on perfection, "hard problems demand that... we relax."
Monte Carlo: When Sampling Beats Thinking

In 1946, mathematician Stanislaw Ulam, recovering from illness and playing solitaire, tried to compute the odds of winning a hand. The combinatorics were hopeless — so he realized he could just deal many hands and count. He and John von Neumann developed this into the Monte Carlo method, using random samples to estimate quantities too complex to derive, and it became indispensable to nuclear physics calculations at Los Alamos. The insight generalizes: when a system has too many interacting possibilities to enumerate, simulating random instances gives you a picture no analysis could.

Randomness also powers algorithms with hard guarantees. Modern cryptography needs enormous prime numbers, but definitively proving primality of huge numbers was long impractical. The Miller-Rabin primality test (Gary Miller, Michael Rabin) instead performs a randomized check: each round, a composite number has at most a one-in-four chance of slipping through. Run the test k times and the false-positive probability falls below (1/4)^k — a few dozen rounds pushes error below one in a septillion, far less likely than a hardware glitch. Every secure web connection you open rests on primes certified only "almost certainly," and that's fine.

The same spirit yields the Bloom filter (Burton Bloom, 1970), a data structure that answers "have I seen this before?" using a fraction of the memory an exact record would need. It hashes each item into several positions in a bit array; a lookup can produce rare false positives but never false negatives. Search engines use Bloom filters to avoid re-crawling URLs, and browsers have used them to flag malicious sites. The trade is explicit: tolerate a small, tunable error rate and previously impossible scales become routine.
Hill Climbing and the Trap of the Local Maximum

Many intractable problems yield to a simple strategy: start with any solution and make small improvements. To improve a tour, swap the order of two cities; keep any swap that shortens it; repeat. This is hill climbing — always stepping uphill in solution quality. Its fatal flaw is the local maximum: a solution that no single small change can improve, yet which may be far from the global best. The landscape of possible solutions is rugged, full of minor peaks that trap pure improvers.

Three escapes, in increasing sophistication. First, jitter: when stuck, make a few random small changes — even if they hurt — then resume climbing from the shaken position. Second, random-restart hill climbing: repeatedly scrap everything, start from a completely random solution, and climb again, keeping the best peak found across restarts; this works remarkably well when good solutions are scattered widely, and it's the standard approach in problems like decrypting simple ciphers. Third, and most elegantly, the Metropolis algorithm (born of the same Los Alamos milieu as Monte Carlo): consider random changes, always accept improvements, and accept bad moves sometimes — with probability that shrinks the worse the move is. Occasional deliberate steps downhill keep the search from freezing on a minor summit while still spending most of its time in good territory.

The human parallel is worth taking seriously. If your routine, career, or portfolio is merely "un-improvable by any small tweak," that certifies only a local maximum. Occasionally accepting a change you'd normally reject is not irrationality — it's the only known way to test whether a taller peak exists elsewhere.
Simulated Annealing: Start Wild, Cool Slowly

In the early 1980s, IBM physicists Scott Kirkpatrick and C. Daniel Gelatt faced chip layout — arranging circuit components to minimize wiring, a brutally hard optimization problem ruled by expert intuition. As physicists, they saw an analogy in annealing: metallurgists get strong, low-energy crystal structures not by cooling metal quickly (which locks in defects) but by heating it and cooling it slowly, letting atoms explore before settling. They gave the Metropolis algorithm a temperature: run the search "hot" at first, accepting even large steps backward, then gradually lower the temperature so the search becomes an ordinary hill climber that finally accepts only improvements.

This simulated annealing — published in Science in 1983 — promptly beat IBM's expert human chip designers and became one of the most practical methods known for NP-hard optimization, used everywhere from circuit layout to airline scheduling. Its deepest lesson is about ordering: randomness belongs at the beginning, rigor at the end. Front-load your wildest experimentation when commitments are few, and become progressively harder to dislodge as the design matures — a schedule that applies as well to careers and relationships as to silicon.

The creative world discovered the same principle independently. Musician Brian Eno and artist Peter Schmidt created Oblique Strategies (1975), a deck of cards bearing cryptic instructions ("Honor thy error as a hidden intention") drawn at random to jolt stuck studio sessions. Eno's insight was exactly the local-maximum diagnosis: comfortable competence is a trap, and an arbitrary external shock forces exploration no deliberate choice would make. Random stimuli — a shuffled card, a random Wikipedia article, an unplanned detour — work not despite being irrelevant but because they're irrelevant: they sample the solution space at a distance no incremental thinking reaches. When truly stuck, don't push harder uphill; add heat.
In practice

When a decision problem feels combinatorially explosive (seating charts, itineraries, schedules), stop seeking the perfect answer — it's likely NP-hard — and explicitly aim for "provably good enough."

Relax one constraint: solve the version of your problem where a hard rule is removed or fractional answers are allowed, then round back to reality; use the relaxed answer as a benchmark for how much the constraint is costing you.
Convert impossible "musts" into prices: list your conflicting obligations, assign each a violation penalty, and optimize the total — Lagrangian relaxation for your calendar.
Estimate by sampling instead of analyzing: to judge odds or forecasts too tangled to compute, simulate ten or a hundred random scenarios and count outcomes.
Escape local maxima on purpose: when a routine or project can't be improved by small tweaks, inject one random change you'd normally reject, or do a full random restart, and climb again.

Schedule your randomness like an annealer: be maximally experimental early in any new job, project, or search, and deliberately reduce your tolerance for wild moves as it matures; when stuck creatively, use a random prompt (an Oblique Strategies card, a random article) to force a distant jump.
